text: Mel Giedroyc (Series 4) is the only contestant to win the same prize twice — Ray's shampoo bag.
The Taskmaster house is actually a former groundskeeper's cottage on a golf course in Chiswick, west London.
Alex Horne never intended to be the Taskmaster himself — he always wanted to be on the ground with contestants.
Bob Mortimer (Series 5) filmed some tasks in November and others in May — flowers had bloomed between shoots.
Mel Giedroyc completed all her tasks in roughly a single week — one of the fastest filming stints ever.
The show was originally a live stage show at the Edinburgh Festival Fringe before becoming a TV series.
Greg Davies' golden head bust that winners receive is made of resin and weighs about 2kg.
There's a task involving popping an entire roll of bubble wrap that has been tried in Series 1, 2, and 3 — but never aired because it made terrible TV.
Frank Skinner agreed to do Series 1, which Alex Horne credits as a major stamp of approval that helped recruit future contestants.
The Taskmaster crew is surprisingly small — only about 15–20 people work behind the scenes.
Alex Horne comes up with the majority of tasks himself, though the small crew pitches in ideas too.
Contestants are kept in a tiny green room between tasks — described as 'fundamentally rock 'n' roll' with a jar of sweets and a fridge.
Each series, the Taskmaster house is redecorated with a different theme inspired by a specific artist.
The studio tasks are always tested beforehand, but location tasks are almost never tried out before filming.
Romesh Ranganathan nearly choked on a watermelon on the very first day of filming Series 1.
Ed Gamble (Series 9 winner) is the only contestant who went on to co-host the Taskmaster podcast.
Jo Brand (Series 9) famously chose a caravan holiday for the entire cast as the final prize — nobody wanted it.
James Acaster (Series 7) scored the lowest ever points in a single episode with just 2 points.
Lee Mack (Series 11) won 7 out of 10 episodes — the highest episode win rate in show history at the time.
The show moved from Dave to Channel 4 starting with Series 10.
Bob Mortimer's Fuji 9 segment and 'Do we need em?' became one of the most beloved moments in Taskmaster history.
There have been over 45 international versions of Taskmaster, including in Norway, Sweden, and New Zealand.
The Taskmaster house garden has been the site of over 500 individual task attempts across all series.
Liza Tarbuck (Series 6 winner) was known for her creative lateral thinking, often finding loopholes in tasks.
Rhod Gilbert (Series 7) holds the record for the most heated arguments with the Taskmaster Greg Davies.
The iconic 'Your time starts now' phrase was inspired by Alex Horne's love of countdown-based games.
Paul Sinha (Series 8) is also a professional quizzer known as 'The Sinnerman' on The Chase.
Noel Fielding (Series 4 winner) brought his signature surreal art style to many creative tasks.
Sarah Kendall (Series 11 winner) was the first Australian contestant on the show.
The Champion of Champions specials bring back series winners to compete against each other for the ultimate title.
Josh Widdicombe won the very first series of Taskmaster and set the bar for future champions.
Tim Key (Series 1) is a poet as well as a comedian — he brought a uniquely literary approach to tasks.
Roisin Conaty (Series 1) accidentally left a potato in her handbag for weeks during a task.
Joe Wilkinson (Series 2) was disqualified from a task for stepping over a line — one of the show's most infamous moments.
Richard Osman (Series 2) is 6'7" tall, making him one of the tallest contestants in the show's history.
Katherine Ryan (Series 2) was the first Canadian contestant on the show.
Rob Beckett (Series 3 winner) credits his competitive nature for carrying him to victory.
Dave Gorman (Series 3) is known for his data-driven comedy — a skill that sometimes helped with analytical tasks.
Hugh Dennis (Series 4) was one of the oldest contestants when he appeared and brought dad-joke energy to every task.
Joe Lycett (Series 4) legally changed his name to Hugo Boss in a publicity stunt before appearing on the show.
Aisling Bea (Series 5) was the first Irish contestant on Taskmaster.
Sally Phillips (Series 5) famously tried to befriend the Taskmaster's assistant during tasks.
Nish Kumar (Series 5) is known for being one of the most enthusiastic but least successful contestants.
Russell Howard (Series 6) brought boundless energy and often tried physical solutions to creative tasks.
Tim Vine (Series 6) holds a Guinness World Record for the most jokes told in an hour.
Phil Wang (Series 7) famously asked philosophical questions about whether something counted as completing a task.
Kerry Godliman (Series 7 winner) was praised for her consistent, methodical approach to tasks.
Iain Stirling (Series 8) is the voice of Love Island — he brought narration-style humour to his tasks.
Sian Gibson (Series 8) is best known for co-starring in Peter Kay's Car Share.
Lou Sanders (Series 8 winner) had one of the most unpredictable and chaotic approaches to tasks.
Rose Matafeo (Series 9) won the Edinburgh Comedy Award the same year she appeared on Taskmaster.
David Baddiel (Series 9) admitted to being terrible at practical tasks but excelled at prize tasks.
Mike Wozniak (Series 11) became a fan favourite for his gentlemanly demeanour and unexpected task solutions.
Charlotte Ritchie (Series 11) revealed that filming tasks took about five solid days of shooting.
Victoria Coren Mitchell (Series 12) is the only person to win two separate televised poker tournaments.
Alan Davies (Series 12) is best known as the permanent panelist on QI for over 19 years.
Dara Ó Briain (Series 14 winner) has a degree in mathematical physics from University College Dublin.
Fern Brady (Series 14) wrote a bestselling memoir about growing up autistic before appearing on the show.
Frankie Boyle (Series 15) was known for his darkly creative approaches to the prize tasks.
Jack Dee (Series 18 winner) was the original host of I'm a Celebrity before becoming a Taskmaster champion.
John Robins (Series 17 winner) holds the all-time record for most points in a single series with 192.
Dara O Briain (Series 14) scored a perfect 30 points in a single episode — tied for the highest single-episode score ever.
Mel Giedroyc holds the record for lowest single-episode score: just 3 points, after receiving a -5 penalty for eating chocolate.
Richard Herring (Series 10) holds the record for most episode wins in a single series with 6.
Phil Wang (Series 7) is the only contestant to never finish in the top two in any episode he appeared in.
Mike Wozniak (Series 11) was once disqualified from every single pre-recorded task in one episode.
Greg Davies has assigned over 1,049 tasks across all UK series — and handed out 348 disqualifications (0.33 per task).
Greg Davies has given out only 32 discretionary bonus points across 1,049 tasks — making him one of the stingiest Taskmasters globally.
Taskmaster has been adapted in over 20 countries including Norway (Kongen Befaler), Sweden (Bast i Test), Finland (Suurmestari), and New Zealand.
The Finnish version Suurmestari has won the Golden Venla award for Best Game Show for six consecutive years (2020-2025).
Alex Horne personally visited the set of Taskmaster Iceland and sent them seven completely original tasks for their debut season.
The Danish Taskmaster Lasse Rimmer holds the franchise record for most disqualifications per task at 0.58 — nearly double Greg Davies.
Taskmaster Iceland (Taskmaster Island) debuted in March 2026, making it one of the newest international versions.
Taskmaster Estonia is called Taskmaster - kuninga kasul which translates as by the order of the king.
Rose Matafeo, who competed in Series 9 of the UK show, went on to become the Taskmaster for Taskmaster New Zealand.
The Taskmaster house was almost never a part of the show — Alex originally planned to film at the contestants own homes.
There is a task involving bubble wrap that was tried in Series 1, 2, and 3 but never aired — it just made terrible television.
Only about 15-20 people work behind the scenes on Taskmaster — a remarkably small crew for a major TV show.
Studio tasks are always tested beforehand, but location tasks are almost never tried out before the contestants attempt them.
Each series has a different art theme inspired by a specific artist — Series 6 was themed around M.C. Escher.
Eggs, watermelons, coconuts, and bananas are among the most frequently used objects across all Taskmaster tasks worldwide.
The most common task categories are drawing, throwing things, finding things, and constructing things.
Guz Khan (Series 12) holds the record for most episode wins without winning the overall series — he won 4 episodes.
Mae Martin (Series 15 winner) had the second-highest points per task average in show history at 3.48.
Series 21 and 22 of Taskmaster UK are both confirmed for 2026, making it one of the longest-running panel shows in UK television.
